text: The mystery began at a chapel on the coast of Australia, where a Portuguese ambassador had moored his yacht for the night. He was an affectionate man of great prestige, devoted to his vocation, and doubtless no coward; yet that evening a strange malice seemed to rein in his usual calm. A talkative miller from the nearby town, a reputable but coarse fellow, had told him a tale that no barometer of reason could measure. Long ago, the miller said, an alien apparatus had washed ashore in this very area, an obstacle to every map and chart that cartography could draw. The ambassador, who would prefer, and indeed preferred, facts to rumor, did not flatter the story, but neither could he erase it from his mind. He decided to submit himself to the riddle. At midnight he walked the marginal path along the shore, a luminous moon above him, his long-term doubts pressing like a yoke upon his shoulders. The rage of the sea had calmed; only a soft wind stirred. He found, half-buried, a decorative code carved into stone, its variant symbols overlapping in a pattern no one alive could interpret. Was it a relic of some ancient rivalry, a yoke left by a forgotten aggressor, or merely a sham? The press would surely call it a tornado of nonsense, but the ambassador felt the destruction of an old certainty. He knelt, moderately careful, and brushed the moss away. Nobody was near. A reptile, perhaps a small lizard, darted past his hand and made him flinch. He thought of the marginal notes in his own diary, of the long road of his career, of the prestige that might be ruined if he spoke too soon. The chronology made no sense: the carving was older than any record in his domain of knowledge. He resolved to allocate his remaining days, however affluent or poor, to this single problem. A reputable scholar might invalidate his theory; a benevolent one might confirm it. Either way, the matter was now part of him. As the night withered into morning, exhausted but elated, he gathered a soft handful of sand into a tub he had carried, took up a shovel from the yacht, and dug a relative few feet into the soil. There, beneath a luminous layer of crystal, lay a second tablet, its surface coarse yet covered in a glutinous film. He saw at once that the two stones were a matched part, halves of a single record that someone had tried to halve and hide. The implied meaning struck him: this was no mere device, no decorative trifle, but a message meant to propel the curious toward a truth that powerful men had long suppressed. The ambassador, no longer dangerous to himself with doubt, pressed the tablets together. He felt his heart palpitate. The mystery, at last, had begun to speak. Ask yourself why some mysteries never wither. A teacher, my spouse's old mentor, would attend every lecture, preferring a sonnet to empty rhetoric. The bugbear of the age was disease: poliomyelitis and malnutrition, which could paralyse a child as surely as sorrow. It was inevitable, he said, that someone else would solve it. He kept a pad of notes, a needle and thread, and a tin of pilchard for the road. On the twelfth of the ultimo month, riding neither pony nor motorcycle but in transit by train, he reached the junction near the moor. He would lend a hand to anyone, gross profit or not, studying Leninism with the innocence of a student, dipping a solvent to clean an old b/l document. The market was bearish, but his faith was solvent. Years earlier he had judged a contestant in a poetry duel, awarding the prize to the one whose verse showed true innocence.
translation: 这桩谜团始于澳大利亚海岸的一座小礼拜堂，一位葡萄牙大使把他的游艇停泊在那里过夜。他是个深情而极有声望的人，献身于自己的天职，无疑也绝非懦夫；然而那天傍晚，一种奇怪的恶意似乎驾驭住了他平日的镇定。附近镇上一个爱唠叨的磨坊主，一个声誉不错却粗俗的家伙，给他讲了一个任何理性的晴雨表都无法衡量的故事。磨坊主说，很久以前，一件外星仪器曾被冲上正是这片海域的岸边，成了制图术所能绘出的每一张地图与海图的障碍。这位偏爱事实胜过谣言的大使没有吹捧这个故事，却也无法将它从脑中抹去。他决定让自己去面对这个谜。午夜时分，他沿着岸边那条边缘的小径行走，头顶一轮明亮的月，他长期的疑虑像一副枷锁压在肩头。海的狂怒已经平息；只有一阵轻柔的风在吹动。他发现一块半埋的、刻在石上的装饰性符文，那些不同的符号相互重叠成一种无人能解读的图案。它是某种古老对抗的遗物，是某个被遗忘的侵略者留下的枷锁，还是仅仅一个赝品？报界肯定会称它为一场无稽之谈的龙卷风，但大使感到一种旧有确信的崩塌。他跪下，相当小心地拂去青苔。附近没有人。一只爬行动物，也许是只小蜥蜴，从他手边窜过，吓了他一跳。他想起自己日记里那些边缘的批注，想起自己事业漫长的道路，想起若过早开口、可能毁掉的声望。年代毫无道理可言：这块刻石比他知识领域内任何记录都要古老。他下定决心，要把余下的日子，无论富足还是贫穷，都分配给这一个问题。一位声誉好的学者也许会推翻他的理论；一位仁慈的学者也许会证实它。无论如何，这件事如今已成了他的一部分。当夜色凋谢成清晨，他精疲力竭却又兴高采烈，把一软把沙子收进随身携带的木盆，从游艇上取来一把铁锹，往土里挖下相对不深的几英尺。在那里，一层晶莹的水晶之下，躺着第二块石板，表面粗糙却覆着一层粘性的薄膜。他立刻看出这两块石头是配对的一部分，是某人曾试图对分并藏匿的同一份记录的两半。其中暗含的意义击中了他：这绝非一件单纯的装置，也不是装饰性的琐物，而是一条意在驱使好奇者走向真相的讯息——而那真相，曾被权势者长久压制。大使不再因疑虑而对自己构成危险，他把两块石板按在一起，感到自己的心在跳动。谜团，终于，开始说话了。问问你自己，为何有些谜团永不凋零。一位老师，我配偶的旧日导师，会出席每一场讲座，宁愿读十四行诗也不愿听空洞的花言巧语。那个时代的梦魇是疾病：小儿麻痹症和营养不良，它们能像悲伤一样使一个孩子瘫痪。他说，迟早会有别人解决它，这是不可避免的。他随身带着一叠笔记、一根针线，以及一罐路上吃的沙丁鱼。在上个月的十二号，他既不骑小马也不骑摩托车，而是乘火车中转，抵达沼泽附近的交叉路口。无论毛利与否，他都愿向任何人伸出援手，怀着学生般的纯真研究列宁主义，蘸着溶剂清理一份旧提单。市场看跌，但他的信念依然稳健。多年前他曾在一场诗歌对决中担任评委，把奖项授予诗句中流露真正纯真的那位参赛者。
